_meta.issue: #122 [M16] Splitting the Holak Scale diagnosis — PERSONAL dimension (v2.1p)
_meta.interaction: Personal maturity self-check (kind: maturity-check) — NON-gating, neutral framing
_meta.source: AI adoption maturity model „Holak Scale”: personal dimension v2.1p (individual, 0–11). Attribution: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka-private.
_meta.dataPolicy: ALL self-check statements are SYNTHETIC and generic (no real data/PII). An indicative diagnosis — it cannot be „failed”. Scoring heuristic is simplified (count of checks → level 0–11) and subject to subject-matter-expert (SME) review.

intro: The Holak Scale's personal dimension (v2.1p) helps you name the maturity of your own way of working with AI — where you are today and what the realistic next step is. It's a self-check, not a test: there is no „pass/fail” result, just an indicative level 0–11 and a hint on how to move up. You can take it independently of the organizational diagnosis (MSHO).

screens.title: What the personal Holak Scale is

blocks.text: The personal version (v2.1p) describes the maturity of your AI craft across 12 levels (0–11) — from first uses to a conscious, measured, continuously improved practice. It helps avoid two traps: „I use AI, so I'm mature” and „it's just a toy”.

items: You assess only YOUR practice — regardless of how mature the organization is.
What matters is HOW you use AI (context, verification, reversibility), not the number of tools.
You take the organizational diagnosis (Holak Scale v2.1e) separately in module MSHO.

blocks.title: Where the name and the model come from
blocks.text: The Holak Scale was devised by Grzegorz Holak with support from Konrad Gomulski — hence the name. Both are Senior Test Development Engineers at Sii Poland and members of SJSI, and co-founders of quality-blog.eu; Grzegorz Holak is additionally a Test Architect and AI Ambassador at SCIB. We use it as a self-reflection tool, not a certification.

screens.title: How to tell your level: axes and two thresholds

blocks.text: You recognize higher levels not by the number of tools, but by habits (from roughly level 4 up): deliberate prompt framing, managing context and cost, verifying critical answers against a source, designing work so decisions stay reversible, and measuring AI's real impact on your quality and time.

items: Barrier — the habit whose absence blocks moving up.
Success — what truly works once the level is reached.
Trap — a false sense of maturity that sets development back.

blocks.title: Two critical thresholds (personal)
blocks.text: Threshold 8→9: from „capable user” to designing work so AI-supported decisions are checkable and reversible, and sharing practices. Threshold 10→11: from routine to measuring your own AI impact and continuously improving your craft.

Next you'll check the statements that are true today for your own practice. The number of checks gives an indicative level 0–11 plus a „where you are / how to move up” hint. Check honestly only what you actually do day to day.

blocks.title: It's a diagnosis, not a grade
blocks.text: There are no correct or incorrect answers here and no pass threshold. The result does not affect path completion or any test — it's a mirror, not an exam.

interaction.title: Holak Scale v2.1p — personal maturity self-check
interaction.intro: Check the statements true today for your own way of working with AI. After checking you'll see an indicative level 0–11 and a next step — with no pass/fail grading.

scales.label: Individual (Holak Scale v2.1p)
scales.intro: How mature is your own way of working with AI? Check what you do day to day.

statements.text: I use AI for specific, repeatable tasks.
I deliberately frame the prompt: role, goal, context, output format.
I recognize that output can be wrong despite a credible form.
I verify critical answers against a source before I use them.
I match the tool/model to the task; I don't use one for everything.
I manage context: I provide only what's needed and watch the cost.
I know when NOT to use AI: missing data, high risk, irreversibility.
I design work so AI-supported decisions are reversible and checkable.
I share practices and calibrate output assessment together with others.
I measure AI's real impact on my quality and time, and adjust my approach.
I treat working with AI as continuous improvement of my craft.

bands.label: Level 0–2: First uses
bands.advice: Barrier: treating AI as an oracle. Next step: start framing prompts deliberately and verifying every answer you intend to use.

bands.label: Level 3–5: Conscious user
bands.advice: Success: you verify output and match the tool to the task. Next step: learn to manage context and cost, and to recognize when NOT to use AI.

bands.label: Level 6–8: Practitioner
bands.advice: Success: you know the limits and manage context. Threshold 8→9: design work so decisions are reversible and checkable, and share practices with your team.

bands.label: Level 9–11: Craft mastery
bands.advice: Trap: routine without measurement or reflection. Threshold 10→11: measure AI's real impact on your work and treat your craft as continuous improvement.

points: The Holak Scale v2.1p measures HOW you use AI (context, verifiability, reversibility), not the number of tools.
You assess only your own practice — the organization is diagnosed separately (MSHO).
It's an indicative diagnosis: no pass/fail, no effect on completion — it's there to point out the next step.
checklist: Have you named your indicative personal level (0–11)?
Do you know the nearest barrier to overcome?
Do you know one concrete next step to move up?
summary.nextStep: Next step: pick one barrier from a higher level and plan a single small, reversible experiment in your own work.
